mnemonics.title: GAUSS
mnemonics.content: G - Gaussian distribution assumption | A - Assume conditional independence | U - Use probability density | S - Sum log-posteriors | S - Select class with maximum posterior

mnemonics.title: NAIVE
mnemonics.content: N - Normal (Gaussian) distribution for continuous | A - Attributes are independent given class | I - Inverse probability (Bayes theorem) | V - Variance & Mean estimated from data | E - Evidence (normalizing constant) computed

acronyms.expansion: Maximum Likelihood Estimation - method to estimate μ (mean) and σ² (variance) from training data
acronyms.usage: Used in Gaussian NB to find optimal parameters that maximize the probability of observing training samples

acronyms.expansion: Maximum A Posteriori - alternative to MLE that incorporates prior beliefs about parameter values
acronyms.usage: Used when we have prior knowledge about feature distributions within classes

acronyms.expansion: Linear Discriminant Analysis - closely related to Gaussian NB but with shared covariance matrix
acronyms.usage: When covariance matrices are shared across classes, Gaussian NB becomes equivalent to LDA

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a bell curve - taller/narrower for low variance (high certainty), shorter/wider for high variance (low certainty). The area under curve always equals 1.
Think of a balance scale: likelihood (from data) × prior (belief before seeing data) = posterior (updated belief). The heavier side wins.
Draw features as separate circles. In Naïve Bayes, they don't overlap (independent). In reality, they may overlap, but the classifier still works.

analogies.analogy: Like a doctor diagnosing based on multiple symptoms. Each symptom (feature) gives likelihood of a disease. Even if symptoms aren't truly independent (headache + fatigue might correlate), the doctor still combines them. Naïve Bayes does the same mathematically.

analogies.analization: Think of height along a mountain range. The PDF tells you the 'elevation' (density) at any point, not the probability of being exactly at that point (which is infinitesimally small for continuous variables).

analogies.analogy: If variance is high, the feature is like a scatterbrained person - their values jump around a lot, so any single observation tells you little. Low variance means they're predictable - one observation tells you a lot.
